merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation at Seoul were established when Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary Lucius H. Foote presented his credentials to the King of Korea.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation at Seoul were established on May 20, 1883, when Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary Lucius H. Foote presented his credentials to the King of Korea.

merged_events.event_description: Following Japan's assumption of direction of Korean foreign relations, U.S. Envoy Edwin Morgan closed the Legation at Seoul.

evidence.quote: Japan assumed direction of Korean foreign relations on November 17, 1905, and U.S. Envoy Edwin Morgan closed the Legation on November 28, 1905.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. Legation closed after Japan assumed direction of Korean foreign relations; Minister Morgan closed the Legation on November 28, 1905.

evidence.quote: Japan assumed direction of Korean foreign relations on 1905-11-17. Note: Morgan closed the Legation on November 28, 1905, and left Korea on December 8, 1905.

merged_events.event_description: Embassy established when John Joseph Muccio presented credentials as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary, the first U.S. Ambassador to Korea, following the post-WWII restoration of Korean independence.

evidence.quote: John Joseph Muccio, Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1949-04-07. Credentials presented: 1949-04-20. Left Korea 1952-09-08.

matched.notes: Exact match on date (1883-05-20) and status (Legation). RDCR confirms Legation established when Minister Foote presented credentials.

CSV date 1905-11-28 matches RDCR Event 1 (closure of Legation by Morgan on Nov 28). POCOM Event 2 uses 1905-11-17, which is the date Japan assumed direction of Korean foreign relations, not the actual closure date. CSV correctly follows RDCR's explicit institutional closure date.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Embassy established when Ambassador John Joseph Muccio presented credentials as the first U.S. Ambassador to Korea, following post-WWII restoration of Korean independence.
missing_from_csv.notes: From POCOM only. This represents the post-WWII re-establishment of U.S. diplomatic relations with Korea at Embassy level. The RDCR narrative for Korea does not appear to cover this event, possibly because the Republic of Korea is tracked under a separate country entry. Verify whether this event belongs under 'Korea' or a separate 'Korea (South)' / 'Republic of Korea' entry before adding.
